title: Building a Research Website with Quarto
text:
Welcome
Welcome! This tutorial will guide you through building your own personal research website using Quarto — a free, open-source tool that lets you create beautiful websites, documents, and reports directly from code.
Don’t worry if you’ve never built a website before, or if you’ve never used R or RStudio. This tutorial starts from the very beginning and walks you through every step. By the end, you’ll have a fully working, professional-looking research website that you can share with the world.
In this tutorial, you will:
This tutorial is designed for complete beginners, but it uses a real, professional workflow. The skills you learn here are the same ones researchers, scientists, and developers use to build and maintain their own websites.
crumbs: 1  Building a Research Website with Quarto

title: Building a Research Website with Quarto
text:
Install R and RStudio — R is a free programming language widely used in research and data science. RStudio is a beginner-friendly application that makes working with R much easier. We’ll walk you through installing both from scratch.
Download a working template from GitHub — Instead of building a website from a blank page, you’ll start with a real, pre-built research website template. GitHub is a free platform where people share and store code — think of it like Google Drive, but for projects.
Run the website on your own computer — Before publishing anything online, you’ll preview your website locally (meaning only you can see it on your computer). This is a safe space to experiment and make mistakes.
Edit the content and structure — You’ll learn how to change text, add your own information, and reorganize pages to reflect your work and research.
Customize the styling — You’ll make the website look like yours by adjusting colors, fonts, and layout.
Publish your site online — Finally, you’ll put your site on the internet so anyone can visit it — for free.



Template source: This tutorial is based on a real research website template. You can view and download the template here: github.com/vmahan1998/Website_Development_Tutorial







Note



This is not just a demo. You will be editing an actual working template used for research communication. Every change you make is a real change to a real website — so you’ll leave this tutorial with something you can actually use and build on.
crumbs: 1  Building a Research Website with Quarto

title: Building a Research Website with Quarto
text:
What You’ll Build
By the end of this tutorial, you’ll have a multi-page personal research website. Here’s a preview of what it will include:







Page
Description




Homepage
An introduction to who you are and what you do


Learning Tab
A space to share resources, notes, or educational content


Research Overview
A summary of your research interests and focus areas


Publications
A list of your papers, reports, or written work


Projects / Models
Highlights of specific projects or tools you’ve developed


CV Page
A digital version of your curriculum vitae (résumé)
crumbs: 1  Building a Research Website with Quarto

title: Building a Research Website with Quarto
section: What You Need Before You Start
text:
What You Need Before You Start
You don’t need to install anything right now — the first chapters will walk you through setup step by step. But here’s a quick overview of what you’ll be working with:

R — A free programming language. Even if you never write a single line of R code yourself, it powers the tools that build your website behind the scenes.
Quarto — The tool that turns your content into a website. It’s free and works alongside R and RStudio.
RStudio — A free application that gives you a visual, beginner-friendly environment for working with R and Quarto. Think of it as your “home base” for this whole project.
A GitHub account — A free account on github.com that you’ll use to store your website files and publish your site online. If you don’t have one yet, we’ll show you how to create one.







Tip



Never used any of these tools before? That’s completely okay — and actually expected! Each tool is introduced gradually, with explanations along the way. You do not need any prior coding or web development experience.
crumbs: 1  Building a Research Website with Quarto

title: Building a Research Website with Quarto
text:
How This Tutorial Works
This tutorial is organized into chapters, and each chapter builds on the one before it. It’s important to follow the chapters in order, especially at the beginning, since later steps assume you’ve completed the earlier ones.
As you go, you’ll be making real edits to the template — not just reading about how things work. This hands-on approach is intentional: the best way to learn is by doing.






Important



Go at your own pace. There’s no rush. If something doesn’t work the first time, that’s normal — troubleshooting is a natural part of working with any new tool. Each chapter includes tips to help you if you get stuck.


Ready to get started? Head to Chapter 1 to install R and RStudio.
crumbs: 1  Building a Research Website with Quarto

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
2.1 Overview
Before you can build or edit your website, you need to set up a few free tools on your computer. This chapter walks you through every installation step and gets the website template running on your machine for the first time.
By the end of this chapter, you will have:

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
R installed on your computer
RStudio installed and open
Quarto installed
The website template downloaded and running in your browser







Note



These installations only need to be done once. Once your environment is set up, you won’t need to repeat these steps.

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
2.2 Step 1: Install R
R is a free programming language that powers the tools behind your website. Even if you never write R code yourself, it needs to be installed for Quarto and RStudio to work properly.

Go to the official R download page: https://cran.r-project.org
Under “Download and Install R”, click the link that matches your operating system:

Windows → click “Download R for Windows” → then “base” → then the download link at the top
Mac → click “Download R for macOS” → choose the version that matches your Mac
Linux → select your distribution and follow the instructions

Open the downloaded file and follow the installer prompts. The default settings are fine — you don’t need to change anything.







Tip



Not sure which version to download? If you’re on a newer Mac (2020 or later), you likely have an Apple Silicon chip — look for the file labeled arm64. If you’re on an older Mac or a Windows PC, the standard download is the right choice.

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
2.3 Step 2: Install RStudio
RStudio is a free application that gives you a visual, beginner-friendly environment for working with R and Quarto. Think of it as the workspace where everything comes together.






Important



You must install R before installing RStudio. RStudio requires R to be present on your computer in order to run.



Go to the RStudio download page: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop
Click “Download RStudio Desktop” — the free version is all you need.
Choose the installer for your operating system, download it, and run it.
Follow the installer prompts. Again, the default settings are fine.
Once installed, open RStudio. You should see a window divided into several panels.







Tip



What am I looking at? When RStudio opens for the first time, you’ll see a few panels: the Console (where R runs commands), the Environment (where your data and variables live), and the Files panel (where you browse your project files). Don’t worry about mastering these right away — you’ll get familiar with them as you go.

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
2.4 Step 3: Install Quarto
Quarto is the tool that actually builds your website. It reads your content files and turns them into the web pages visitors will see. You’ll install it directly from inside RStudio using the R Console — no need to visit any external websites or run a separate installer.






Note



The Console is the tab in the bottom-left panel of RStudio where you type and run R commands. It usually has a > symbol waiting for your input. This is different from the Terminal tab next to it — the Console is for R code, while the Terminal is for system-level commands.



Open RStudio
Click on the Console tab in the bottom-left panel
Click next to the > symbol and type the following command, then press Enter:

install.packages("quarto")

R will download and install the Quarto package. You may see a lot of text scroll by — that’s normal. Wait until you see the > prompt appear again, which means it’s done.
Once installed, load the package by typing the following and pressing Enter:

library(quarto)

To confirm everything installed correctly, run:

quarto_path()
If a file path is printed in the Console (for example, /usr/local/bin/quarto), Quarto is installed and ready to go.






Tip



Seeing a warning that says the package was built under a different version of R? That’s usually fine and won’t affect anything in this tutorial. As long as the > prompt returns without an error message in red, you’re good to continue.








Important



If you see a red error message saying Quarto was not found or the path is empty, it may mean Quarto’s command-line tools are not installed on your system yet. In that case, visit https://quarto.org/docs/get-started, download the installer for your operating system, and run it. Then return to this step and run quarto_path() again to confirm.

title: 2  Getting Started
section: 2.5 Step 4: Create a Free GitHub Account
text:
2.5 Step 4: Create a Free GitHub Account
GitHub is a free platform where people store and share code and projects. You’ll use it to download the website template and later to publish your site online.
If you already have a GitHub account, you can skip to Step 5.

Go to https://github.com and click “Sign up”
Follow the prompts to create a free account.
Verify your email address when prompted.







Tip



Choose a professional username if you can — your GitHub profile may be visible to collaborators or employers in the future.

title: 2  Getting Started
text:
2.6 Step 5: Download the Website Template
You’ll download the template directly from GitHub as a ZIP file — no special tools or commands needed for this step, just your web browser.

Go to the template repository in your browser: https://github.com/vmahan1998/Personal_Website_Template
Click the green “Code” button near the top-right of the page
In the dropdown that appears, click “Download ZIP”
Your browser will download a file called Personal_Website_Template-main.zip
Once downloaded, unzip the file:

Windows: Right-click the ZIP file → select “Extract All” → choose a destination (Desktop or Documents is fine) → click “Extract”
Mac: Double-click the ZIP file — it will automatically unzip into a folder in the same location

You should now have a folder called Personal_Website_Template-main containing all the website files







Tip



Move the unzipped folder somewhere easy to find and remember — you’ll be opening and editing files inside it throughout this tutorial. Avoid leaving it in your Downloads folder where it can get buried.








Note



What’s inside the folder? Don’t worry if the contents look unfamiliar right now. The next chapter will walk you through what each file and folder does. For now, you just need the folder in place and ready to open.

title: 2  Getting Started
section: 2.7 Step 6: Open the Project in RStudio
text:
2.7 Step 6: Open the Project in RStudio
Now you’ll open the template as a project in RStudio. Opening it as a project (rather than just opening individual files) is important — it tells RStudio where all your files live so that everything works correctly together.

Open RStudio
Go to File → Open Project
Navigate to the Personal_Website_Template-main folder you just unzipped
Inside the folder, find the file ending in .Rproj and click on it
Click “Open”

RStudio will reload and you’ll see all the template files appear in the Files panel in the bottom-right corner of the screen.






Note



The .Rproj file is what tells RStudio to treat this folder as a self-contained project. It sets up your working environment automatically so all the files and folder paths work correctly without any extra configuration on your part.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.1 Overview
Now that your project is open in RStudio, you’re probably looking at a list of files and wondering what they all do. This chapter explains how a Quarto website is organized, what each file’s role is, and how they connect to produce the website you previewed in the last chapter.
Understanding the structure before you start editing will save you a lot of confusion later — so take a few minutes to read through this before making any changes.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.2 The Big Picture
A Quarto website is just a folder of plain text files. When you run quarto preview or quarto render, Quarto reads those files and converts them into the HTML pages that make up your website.
Here’s a map of what’s in your project folder and what each piece does:
Personal_Website_Template/
│
├── _quarto.yml          ← The control center: site title, navigation, theme
├── index.qmd            ← Your homepage
├── research.qmd         ← Your research overview page
├── publications.qmd     ← Your publications list
├── projects.qmd         ← Your projects and models page
├── cv.qmd               ← Your CV page
├── modeling_basics.qmd  ← A learning/tutorial tab
│
├── custom.scss          ← Advanced style customization (colors, fonts)
├── styles.css           ← Additional styling rules
│
├── images/              ← Folder for photos and figures used on the site
├── files/               ← Folder for downloadable files like your CV PDF
├── docs/                ← Auto-generated folder; this is what gets published online
│
└── *.Rproj              ← RStudio project file (you opened this in Chapter 1)






Note



You don’t need to understand every file right now. Focus on the .qmd files and _quarto.yml for now — those are where almost all of your editing will happen.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.3 The .qmd Files — Your Page Content
Files ending in .qmd are Quarto Markdown files. Each one becomes a single page on your website. They are plain text files, which means you can open and edit them in RStudio like a document.
Here’s what each page in your template is for:








File
Page
What it contains




index.qmd
Homepage
Your name, photo, research summary, and featured tools


research.qmd
Research
An overview of your research areas and dissertation work


publications.qmd
Publications
A list of your papers, reports, and written work


projects.qmd
Projects / Models
Highlights of tools and projects you’ve built


cv.qmd
CV
A web version of your curriculum vitae


modeling_basics.qmd
Learning Tab
Educational content or resources you want to share



If you open any of these files in RStudio, you’ll see they follow the same basic pattern: a short header block at the top (called the YAML front matter, enclosed in ---), followed by regular text with some formatting.
For example, at the top of research.qmd you’ll see:
---
title: "Research"
---
That’s all Quarto needs to know the title of the page. Everything below that line is the content that appears on the page.






Tip



What is Markdown? Markdown is a simple way of formatting text using plain characters. For example, putting **text** around a word makes it bold, and starting a line with ## makes it a heading. You don’t need to learn all of Markdown right now — you’ll pick it up naturally as you edit the template pages.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
section: 3.4 The _quarto.yml File — Your Site’s Control Center
text:
3.4 The _quarto.yml File — Your Site’s Control Center
The _quarto.yml file is the most important configuration file in your project. It controls the overall structure of the site: the title, the navigation bar, the theme, the footer, and more.
Open it in RStudio now and take a look. Here’s what the key sections mean:

3.4.1 Site Title
website:
  title: "Vanessa Quintana"
This sets the name that appears in the browser tab and the top-left of the navigation bar. You’ll change this to your own name when you personalize the site.


3.4.2 Navigation Bar
navbar:
  left:
    - href: modeling_basics.qmd
      text: Modeling Basics
    - href: research.qmd
      text: Research
    - href: publications.qmd
      text: Publications
    - href: projects.qmd
      text: Models
    - href: cv.qmd
      text: CV
This is where the navigation bar links are defined. Each entry has two parts: - href — the .qmd file this link points to - text — the label that appears in the navbar
The order here controls the order the links appear on your site. If you want to rename a tab or reorder the pages, this is where you do it.


3.4.3 Social Links (Right Side of Navbar)
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/vmahan1998
      - icon: envelope
        href: mailto:vanessa.mahan@maine.edu
      - icon: linkedin
        href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/vanessa-mahan/
These are the icon links that appear on the right side of the navigation bar. You’ll update these with your own GitHub, email, and LinkedIn in a later chapter.


3.4.4 Theme
format:
  html:
    theme: [flatly, custom.scss]
    css: styles.css
This tells Quarto which visual theme to use. The template uses flatly (a clean, professional Bootstrap theme) combined with a custom style file (custom.scss) that adds the dark color scheme you saw when you previewed the site.


3.4.5 Output Folder
project:
  output-dir: docs
This tells Quarto to save the built website files into a folder called docs. This matters when it comes time to publish — GitHub Pages looks for your site files in the docs folder by default.






Important



Be careful editing _quarto.yml. It uses YAML formatting, which is very sensitive to indentation. If a line is indented by the wrong number of spaces, Quarto may throw an error. Always use spaces (not tabs) for indentation, and keep the existing structure intact while making your edits.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.5 The Styling Files
Your template has two files that control how the site looks visually:
styles.css — A CSS file that applies the dark background, text colors, card styles, and other visual details across the site. CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the standard language for styling web pages. You don’t need to edit this file to personalize your content, but you’ll explore it in the styling chapter later.
custom.scss — A SCSS file that extends and customizes the base Quarto theme. SCSS is a more powerful version of CSS. Again, you won’t need to touch this right away.






Note



The separation between content (.qmd files) and style (styles.css, custom.scss) is intentional and useful. It means you can completely redesign the look of your site without touching a single word of your content — and vice versa.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.6 The images/ and files/ Folders
images/ is where you store any photos or figures used on the site — your headshot, research images, project screenshots, and so on. When a .qmd file references an image like images/Author_Picture.jpg, Quarto looks for it in this folder.
files/ is where you store downloadable files — most commonly a PDF version of your CV. A link on your CV page points here so visitors can download it.






Tip



When you’re ready to personalize the site, you’ll add your own headshot to the images/ folder and your CV PDF to the files/ folder. You’ll do this in Chapter 3.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.7 The docs/ Folder
The docs/ folder is automatically generated by Quarto when you run quarto render. You should never edit files inside docs/ directly — Quarto will overwrite your changes the next time it builds the site.
Think of docs/ as the final, compiled version of your website. It’s the folder that gets published online when you deploy to GitHub Pages.

title: 3  Understanding the File Structure
text:
3.8 Summary








File / Folder
What it’s for
Do you edit it?




_quarto.yml
Site structure, navigation, theme
Yes — for titles, links, settings


*.qmd files
Page content
Yes — your main editing happens here


styles.css
Visual styling
Later — for design customization


custom.scss
Theme customization
Later — for advanced styling


images/
Photos and figures
Yes — add your own images here


files/
Downloadable files (CV PDF)
Yes — add your CV PDF here


docs/
Auto-generated site output
Never — Quarto manages this


*.Rproj
RStudio project file
Never — leave this as-is



Now that you understand how the pieces fit together, you’re ready to start making the site your own. In the next chapter, you’ll edit your homepage and add your personal information.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.1 Overview
Now that you understand how the files are organized, it’s time to make the website yours. This chapter focuses on editing index.qmd — your homepage — which is the first thing visitors will see.
You’ll learn how to:

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
Replace the placeholder text with your own name and bio
Add your own profile photo
Arrange text and images side by side using columns
Embed a live public Google Calendar







Tip



Before making any edits, it’s a good habit to run quarto preview in the Terminal so the preview is live in your browser. That way you can see your changes reflected in real time as you save the file.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.2 Step 1: Open Your Homepage File
In RStudio, find index.qmd in the Files panel (bottom-right) and click on it to open it in the editor.
At the very top of the file you’ll see the YAML front matter — a short block between two --- lines that sets page-level options:
---
title: "Vanessa Quintana"
subtitle: "Applied Ecological Modeler • Coastal Systems Scientist • Geospatial Analyst"
page-layout: full
image: images/Author_Picture.jpg
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    smooth-scroll: true
---
Start by updating these fields:

title — Replace "Vanessa Quintana" with your own name
subtitle — Replace the subtitle with your own roles or research focus areas, separated by • if you’d like the same style
image — This points to your profile photo (more on this in Step 2)







Note



The page-layout: full setting gives the homepage a wider layout than the other pages, which is why it looks different from research.qmd or cv.qmd. Leave this as-is for the homepage.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.3 Step 2: Add Your Profile Photo
Your profile photo is displayed on the left side of the homepage. To replace the placeholder with your own image:

Find a photo you’d like to use — a headshot or professional photo works best
Rename the file to something simple with no spaces, for example my_photo.jpg
Move or copy the file into the images/ folder inside your project
In index.qmd, find the line that references the image:

![](images/Author_Picture.jpg){.profile-image width="100%" fig-alt="Vanessa Quintana"}

Replace Author_Picture.jpg with your own filename, and update the fig-alt text with your name:

![](images/my_photo.jpg){.profile-image width="100%" fig-alt="Your Name"}






Tip



The fig-alt text is the alt text for your image — a short description that appears if the image fails to load and that screen readers use for accessibility. Always include a brief, accurate description.








Important



Make sure your image file is actually inside the images/ folder and that the filename in your .qmd file matches exactly — including capitalization and the file extension (.jpg, .png, etc.). A mismatch here is one of the most common beginner errors.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.4 Step 3: Edit Your Bio Text
Below the YAML front matter, the homepage is built using columns — a Quarto layout feature that lets you place content side by side. Here’s how the two-column layout is structured in index.qmd:
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="32%"}

  [your photo and profile panel go here]

:::
::: {.column width="68%"}

  [your bio text goes here]

:::
:::
The first column (32% wide) holds your photo and badge list. The second column (68% wide) holds your bio and research philosophy. To update your bio, find the text in the wider column and replace it with your own:
::: {.column width="68%"}

::: {.kicker}
About My Work
:::

I work at the intersection of [your research areas here]. My research focuses on
[what you study and why it matters].

I approach [your methods or philosophy here].

:::






Note



What is .kicker? The .kicker is a small styled label that appears above a section heading — like a category tag. It’s defined in styles.css and gives the page a clean, editorial look. You can change the text inside it to anything you like, or remove it entirely if you prefer.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.5 Step 4: Understanding Side-by-Side Layouts (Columns)
The column layout you just saw is one of the most useful features in Quarto for building a polished homepage. Here’s the full pattern so you can use it anywhere on your site:
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

Content for the left side goes here.

You can put text, images, lists, or anything else.

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

Content for the right side goes here.

:::
:::
The widths must add up to 100%. You can split them however you like — for example 30% / 70%, or 25% / 25% / 25% / 25% for four equal columns (as used in the Research Areas section of the homepage).

4.5.1 Placing an Image Next to Text
A common use of the column layout is putting a photo or figure next to a block of text. Here’s a simple example you can copy and adapt:
::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}

![](images/my_photo.jpg){width="100%" fig-alt="A description of the image"}

:::
::: {.column width="60%"}

### A heading for the text side

Write your paragraph text here. This will appear to the right of the image.
You can include as much or as little text as you need.

:::
:::






Tip



Add a blank line between the ::: dividers and your content. Quarto needs that spacing to correctly parse the column layout — missing blank lines is a common cause of formatting issues.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.6 Step 5: Update Your Badge List
In the left column of the homepage, below your photo, there is a badge list — a set of small styled labels showing your affiliation, title, or research focus areas. Find this block in index.qmd:
::: {.badge-list}
Marine Sciences, University of Maine

ORISE Fellow, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers

Estuarine Hydrodynamics and Sediment Transport

Agent-Based and Ecological Modeling
:::
Replace each line with your own affiliations, department, institution, or keywords. Each separate line becomes its own badge. You can add or remove lines as needed.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
section: 4.7 Step 6: Embed a Public Google Calendar
text:
4.7 Step 6: Embed a Public Google Calendar
If you have a public Google Calendar — for office hours, events, lab meetings, or workshops — you can embed it directly into your website as a live, interactive calendar. Any updates you make in Google Calendar will automatically appear on your site.

4.7.1 First: Make Your Google Calendar Public

Go to calendar.google.com and sign in
In the left panel, find the calendar you want to embed, click the three dots next to its name, and select “Settings and sharing”
Under “Access permissions for events”, check the box that says “Make available to public”
Confirm the warning if prompted



4.7.2 Second: Get the Embed Code

Still on the calendar settings page, scroll down to “Integrate calendar”
Copy the “Public URL to this calendar” — it will look like a long link ending in @group.calendar.google.com
In the same section, click “Customize” to open the embed builder if you’d like to adjust the calendar’s appearance, then copy the <iframe> embed code



4.7.3 Third: Add the Calendar to Your Site (optional)
Create a new file in your project called calendar.qmd and add the following. Replace the src URL with your own calendar’s embed URL:
---
title: "Event Calendar"
toc: true
page-layout: article
---

# Event Calendar

<div style="
  max-width: 1100px;
  margin: 0 auto 50px auto;
  border-radius: 22px;
  background: #ffffff;
  box-shadow: 0 12px 25px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);
  overflow: hidden;
">

  <iframe 
    src="YOUR-GOOGLE-CALENDAR-EMBED-URL-HERE"
    style="border:0; width:100%; height:900px; display:block;"
    frameborder="0"
    scrolling="no"
  ></iframe>

</div>


4.7.4 Fourth: Add the Calendar to Your Navigation Bar (optional)
Open _quarto.yml and add your new calendar page to the navbar under the left: section:
navbar:
  left:
    - href: calendar.qmd
      text: Calendar
    - href: research.qmd
      text: Research
Save _quarto.yml, then run quarto preview in the Terminal to see your calendar page live.






Note



The calendar will only display correctly when viewed in a browser — it won’t show up in RStudio’s built-in viewer panel. If you don’t see it in the RStudio preview pane, copy the local address (e.g. http://localhost:4321) and open it in Chrome or Firefox.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
section: 4.8 Step 7: Save and Preview Your Changes
text:
4.8 Step 7: Save and Preview Your Changes
After making any edits in index.qmd or _quarto.yml, save the file with Ctrl+S (Windows) or Cmd+S (Mac). If quarto preview is already running in the Terminal, your browser should refresh automatically within a few seconds.
If the preview doesn’t update, go to the Terminal and run:
quarto preview






Important



If you see an error in the Terminal after saving, read the message carefully — Quarto’s error messages usually tell you exactly which file and line the problem is on. The most common issues are mismatched ::: column dividers, incorrect image filenames, or indentation errors in _quarto.yml.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
text:
4.9 Other Resources
As you continue building your website, you may want to explore more advanced customization, layouts, and templates. The official Quarto documentation is extremely well organized and worth bookmarking.






Tip



If something feels limited in your website, it almost always already exists in the Quarto documentation — you just need to find the right section.




4.9.1 Website Development

Quarto Websites Documentation
A complete guide to building and structuring Quarto websites, including navigation, pages, and configuration.
Quarto Website Gallery
Real examples of websites built with Quarto. Useful for inspiration and design ideas.
Quarto Guide
A broader introduction to Quarto, including project types, publishing, and workflows.




4.9.2 Customization and Advanced Formatting

HTML Format Reference
Detailed reference for all HTML output options, including themes, layout, and advanced settings.
HTML Basics
A simpler introduction to how Quarto renders HTML, useful for understanding how your site is structured.








Note



You do not need to learn all of this at once. Start by building your site, then return to these resources when you want to customize something specific.





4.9.3 Suggested Workflow for Learning More

Build your basic website using this tutorial

Identify one thing you want to improve (layout, styling, navigation)

Use the documentation above to find that specific feature

Implement and test it







Important



Avoid trying to learn all of Quarto at once. Focus on small, incremental improvements.

title: 4  Editing Your Content
section: 4.10 Summary: What You Edited in This Chapter
text:
4.10 Summary: What You Edited in This Chapter








What
Where
How




Your name and subtitle
index.qmd YAML front matter
Edit the title and subtitle fields


Your profile photo
images/ folder + index.qmd
Add file to images/, update the filename


Your bio text
index.qmd wide column
Replace the paragraph text


Your badge labels
index.qmd .badge-list block
Replace each line with your own info


Side-by-side layout
Any .qmd file
Use the {.columns} / {.column} pattern


Google Calendar
New calendar.qmd + _quarto.yml
Embed iframe, add link to navbar



In the next chapter, you’ll edit the inner pages — Research, Publications, Projects, and CV — and learn how to add your own work to each one.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.1 Overview
Styling controls how your website looks, including colors, fonts, spacing, and layout.
In Quarto, styling happens in two layers:

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
Theme (SCSS) → controls global design such as colors and typography

CSS → fine-tunes specific elements like buttons, spacing, and layout details

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.2 How Styling Works

5.2.1 1. Theme (SCSS)
Defined in _quarto.yml:
format:
  html:
    theme: [flatly, custom.scss]

flatly is the base Bootstrap theme

custom.scss is your custom design layer







Note



SCSS allows you to define variables like colors once and reuse them across your entire site.





5.2.2 2. CSS (styles.css)
css: styles.css
This file overrides specific elements after the theme is applied.
Your current setup includes:

Dark background gradient

Light readable text

Blue accent colors

Styled sidebar and headings

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.3 Your Current Theme: Dark Mode
Your site currently uses a dark, professional theme:

Deep blue background

Light text

Blue accent highlights

This works well for research portfolios and technical content.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.4 Creating Multiple Themes
You can use different styles by switching SCSS files.
Within this template, we provide:

Dark mode

Light mode

Pastel theme







Tip



You can edit these themes or create your own to personalize your website.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.5 Where to Edit Your Style

5.5.1 Open custom.scss
This file controls your core theme settings, including:

Background color

Text color

Accent color

Navbar styling

This is where you define the overall look of your site.



5.5.2 Open _quarto.yml
This file tells Quarto which theme to use.
For example:
format:
  html:
    theme: [flatly, custom.scss]
To switch themes, replace custom.scss with another file:
theme: [flatly, custom-light.scss]
or
theme: [flatly, custom-pastel.scss]

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.6 Editing Colors Step-by-Step
This section walks you through making a simple style change so you can see how themes work in real time.






Important



Make sure you have quarto preview running before you start so you can see your changes instantly.




5.6.1 Step 1: Open custom.scss
In your project folder, open:
custom.scss
You should see something like:
/*-- scss:defaults --*/

$body-bg: #08131d;
$body-color: rgba(222, 238, 246, 0.92);
$primary: #7ecce6;



5.6.2 Step 2: Change the Accent Color
Find this line:
$primary: #7ecce6;
Replace it with a new color, for example:
$primary: #ff6b6b;
Save the file.
👉 Your site should automatically refresh, and you’ll see: - Links change color
- Buttons change color
- Callouts update



5.6.3 Step 3: Change the Background Color
Find:
$body-bg: #08131d;
Try changing it to something lighter:
$body-bg: #f4f7fb;
Save the file.
👉 Notice how the entire tone of the site changes.



5.6.4 Step 4: Adjust Text Color
Find:
$body-color: rgba(222, 238, 246, 0.92);
If you switched to a light background, update this to:
$body-color: #1a1a1a;







Note



Background and text color should always have strong contrast so your site remains readable.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.7 Understanding What You Changed



Variable
What it controls




$primary
Accent color (links, buttons, highlights)


$body-bg
Page background


$body-color
Main text color

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.8 Try It Yourself
Experiment with different combinations:

Blue → Green

Dark → Light

Pastel → Bold

You can use tools like: - https://coolors.co
- https://colorhunt.co







Tip



Small changes go a long way. Changing just one or two variables can completely transform your site.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.9 Common Mistakes

5.9.1 Nothing changes after saving

Make sure quarto preview is running

Check that you’re editing the correct file



5.9.2 Colors look wrong

Your styles.css may be overriding your SCSS



5.9.3 Site looks broken

Check for missing semicolons (;) in SCSS

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.10 Next Step
Now that you’ve changed your first colors, try:

Creating your own theme file

Switching between themes in _quarto.yml

Matching your site to your personal or institutional branding







Important



The goal is not to memorize SCSS — it is to understand how small changes affect your entire site.

title: 5  Customizing Style
text:
5.11 Key Idea

custom.scss = controls design

styles.css = refines details

_quarto.yml = connects everything








Important



If your styling is not updating, check that the file name in _quarto.yml matches your SCSS file exactly.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
6.1 Overview
Once your website is built, the final step is publishing it so others can access it online.
There are two common options:

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
GitHub Pages → best for most users (free, simple, widely used)

Posit Connect → best for organizations, labs, or internal deployments

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.1 Step 1: Prepare Your Project
Make sure your _quarto.yml includes:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs






Important



GitHub Pages requires your site to be rendered into a folder named docs.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.2 Step 2: Render Your Website
In the Terminal, run:
quarto render
This creates a docs/ folder containing your website.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.3 Step 3: Push to GitHub

Create a new repository on GitHub

Upload your project files

Make sure the docs/ folder is included

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.4 Step 4: Enable GitHub Pages
Go to your repository:
Settings → Pages
Then set:

Branch: main

Folder: /docs

Click Save

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.5 Step 5: Access Your Site
Your website will be available at:
https://yourusername.github.io/repository-name/







Tip



It may take 1–2 minutes for your site to go live the first time.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.6 Updating Your Site
Whenever you make changes:

Save your files

Run:

quarto render

Push changes to GitHub

Your site will automatically update.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
7.7 Common Issues

7.7.1 Site not showing

Check that GitHub Pages is enabled

Make sure docs/ exists



7.7.2 Changes not appearing

Wait a minute and refresh

Confirm you pushed updates



7.7.3 Broken links or missing pages

Check file paths

Verify _quarto.yml navigation

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.1 When to Use Posit Connect
Use this option if:

Your organization provides access

You are deploying internal tools

You need authentication or restricted access

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.2 Step 1: Connect Your Account
Your organization will provide:

A Posit Connect URL

Login credentials

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.3 Step 2: Publish from RStudio
In RStudio:

Open your Quarto project

Click Publish

Choose Posit Connect

Select your project

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.4 Step 3: Deploy
RStudio will:

Render your site

Upload it to the server

Provide a live URL

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.5 Step 4: Share Your Site
Your site will be available at a URL like:
https://your-org.connect.server/content/your-site/







Note



Unlike GitHub Pages, Posit Connect can host dynamic content and restrict access to specific users.

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.6 Key Differences



Feature
GitHub Pages
Posit Connect




Cost
Free
Organization-provided


Access
Public
Public or private


Setup
Simple
Requires server access


Best for
Personal websites
Research tools and apps

title: 6  Publishing Your Website
text:
8.7 Summary

GitHub Pages is the best starting point for most users

Posit Connect is ideal for professional or institutional workflows

Both allow you to share your work beyond your local machine








Important



Publishing is what turns your website from a project into a tool others can actually use.
